parts.answer: • Metre rule - 0.1 cm and Vernier calipers - 0.1 mm (1)

• Measure length and width of sheet in multiple positions and obtain mean • Measure thickness for at least 20 sheets • Measure total mass for at least 20 sheets • Calculate density = mass / (length × width × thickness) MP2/3 – reference to number of sheets need only be seen once but the same number of sheets must be used for measurements of thickness and mass. • (Absolute) uncertainty would stay the same Or resolution of the measuring device is the same • So, percentage uncertainty in thickness/mass would reduce MP2 dependent on MP1 (1) (1) Total for question 1 7 Number Answer Mark

• Difficult to identifying when sound was loudest Or Difficulty hearing tuning fork due to background noise • Tube moved when marking the water level Or Tube not vertical when water level was marked (1) (1) • Calculation of the mean using 5 values • Mean l = 18.8 cm to 3 s.f. Example of calculation Mean l = (18.4 + 18.0 + 19.2 + 19.4 + 19.2) / 5 Mean l = 18.8 cm (1) (1) • Use of half the range • Percentage uncertainty = 4 (%) OR • Use of value furthest from the mean (18.0) • Percentage uncertainty = 4 (%) Allow ecf from 2(b)(i) for use of 4 values (e.g. ignoring 18.0) for both mark points. Example of calculation Range = 19.4 – 18.0 = 1.4 cm Percentage uncertainty = (0.7 cm / 18.8 cm)  100 % = 3.7 % Difference from mean = 18.8 – 18.0 = 0.8 cm Percentage uncertainty = (0.8 cm / 18.8 cm)  100 % = 4.3 % (1) (1)
• Use of v = f • Speed of sound = 331 m s –1 Allow e.c.f from 2(b)(i) Example of calculation  = 4  0.188 m = 0.752 m v = 440 Hz  0.752 m = 331 m s –1 (1) (1)

• Use of percentage uncertainty from (b)(ii) to calculate relevant maximum/minimum value for speed of sound from (c) • Statement comparing this with 343 m s –1 MP1 – only needs to calculate one boundary – e.g. maximum if their value in (c) is below 343 m s –1 , minimum if (c) is above 343 m s –1 . OR • Calculates the percentage difference between 343 m s –1 and their speed of sound from (c) • Statement comparing this with their percentage uncertainty from (b)(ii) Example of calculation Percentage uncertainty = 4 % v = 331 m s –1 Max v = 331   =  m s –1 (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 2 10 Number Answer Mark
• Diameter value = 17.90 mm • Use of half resolution (0.005 mm) • Percentage uncertainty = 0.03 (%) Example of calculation Percentage uncertainty = (0.005 mm / 17.90 mm)  100 % = 0.028 % (1) (1) (1)

• Check for zero error Allow do not overtighten (1) • When stationary, the reading on the force meter = weight (− upthrust) • When moving (at a constant speed), the reading on the force meter = weight + drag (− upthrust) • Subtracting the two readings gives the value of drag For MP1 and MP2 – accept descriptions given as an equation e.g. When stationary F = W – U When moving F = W + D − U (1) (1) (1) • Subtracts the two forces (F = 0.09 N) • Use of F = 6πηrv • η = 1.7 (Pa s) Example of calculation F = 0.29 N – 0.20 N F = 0.09 N F = 6πηrv η = F/6πrv η = 0.09 N / (6 × π × 0.00895 m × 0.32 m s –1 ) η = 1.67 Pa s (1) (1) (1)

• A comment assessing uncertainty in force • A comment assessing uncertainty in distance • A comment assessing uncertainty in time • Conclusion justified by their assessments MP4 requires some numerical comparison Examples of assessments for MP1-3 Force • Resolution of the force meter is 0.01 N, so percentage uncertainty is 11% (accept 5.5% or 6%) • Force difficult to keep constant, variation likely to be larger than 0.01 N Distance • Meter rule resolution of 1mm, so percentage uncertainty is small • Percentage uncertainty in distance measurement is 0.2% (accept 0.4%) Time • Resolution of the stopwatch is 0.01 s, so percentage uncertainty is 0.6 % (accept 1.2%) • Time is short, so reaction time (0.2 s) will be a significant percentage (25%) or fraction (1/4) of the time measured • Not enough time to move eyeline, so there may be parallax error when judging when the sphere has passed the rubber band. (1) (1) (1) (1) Total for question 3 14 Number Answer Mark

• Re-arranges equation 푉 푎 = ℎ푐 푒 휆 + 푊 푒 and compares to y = mx + c • Identifies 푔푟푎푑푖푒푛푡= ℎ푐 푒 • States that h, c and e are all constants (1) (1) (1) • Correct values calculated • Values correctly rounded to 3 sig. fig. Example λ / × 10 −7 m 1/λ / × 10 m −1 6.60 1.52 6.12 1.63 5.92 1.69 5.85 1.71 5.30 1.89 4.70 2.13 (1) (1) • Labels axes with quantities and units • Sensible scales • Plotting – 2 points furthest from their line • Plotting – 2 points at the ends • Line of best fit Refer to Mark Scheme Notes – Section 5 for guidance on axis labels, suitable scales & checking accuracy of plots. An example of the graph can be seen on page 11. (1) (1) (1) (1) (1) • Calculates gradient using large triangle - at least half their line of best fit • Use of gradient = hc/e • h = 6.65×10 –34 to 6.85×10 –34 J s Example calculation Gradient = (2.55 – 1.80) V / (2.10 – 1.60)×10 m –1 = 1.25×10 –6 V m h = 1.25×10 –6 V m × 1.60×10 −19 C / 3.00×10 m s –1 = 6.67×10 –34 J s (1) (1) (1) • Mathematical comparison between their value from (b)(iii) and 6.63×10 –34 J s • Comparative statement consistent with MP1 MP2 is for a statement that is justified by their value for h. E.g. Difference between the values is 0.04 (×10 –34 ) is very small compared to 6.63 (×10 –34 ), so method is accurate. Or Percentage difference is 0.6%, which is small, so method is accurate. (1) (1)

• Manufacturer’s wavelength would be shorter (than the wavelength of photons with least energy) Or Manufacturer’s wavelength would be shorter (than the wavelength of photons emitted at V a ) • A lower  would give a higher 1/ Or the line would shift to the right, EITHER • Difference in wavelength would be small, so negligible shift in points (Accept shift would be the same for all points, so same gradient) • No change in the value of h obtained. OR • Points for longer  would shift 1/ values less, decreasing the gradient • Decreasing the value of h obtained.
